metadata.summary: Heuristic evaluation is a usability inspection method where evaluators examine an interface against Nielsen's 10 established usability principles. Quick expert assessment before testing with users; 3-5 independent evaluators find approximately 75% of problems without requiring real users or full testing.

content.title: Heuristic Evaluation — Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics
content.summary: Heuristic evaluation is a usability inspection method where expert evaluators examine an interface against established usability principles (heuristics). Full usability testing is expensive and time-consuming; heuristic evaluation provides quick expert feedback on obvious problems before testing with users. Three to five independent evaluators find approximately 75% of problems; a single evaluator finds only 35%. This methodology applies Nielsen's 10 foundational heuristics: visibility of system status, match between system and real world, user control and freedom, consistency and standards, error prevention, recognition over recall, flexibility and efficiency, aesthetic and minimalist design, error recovery, and help and documentation.

why.p: Full usability testing is expensive and time-consuming. Heuristic evaluation provides quick expert feedback on obvious problems before testing with users, catching known issues early in the design cycle. Three to five independent evaluators find approximately 60–75% of usability problems without requiring user recruitment, scheduling, or payment. Problems obvious to experts should not reach users; catching them before user testing ensures testing resources are spent on deeper insights rather than known patterns. Expert review also provides a measurable, repeatable baseline for design quality.

list.item: Before scheduling usability testing sessions — clear known issues first so sessions surface deeper problems.
When budget or timeline prevents full usability testing.
During rapid iteration cycles where expert review can gate each design sprint.
When evaluating a competitor's interface or an acquired product for UX debt.
As a quality gate before handoff from design to development.
As the sole validation method before launch — expert review does not substitute for real user testing.
When you need to understand "why" users struggle, not just "what" violates heuristics.
For novel interaction paradigms with no established heuristics (e.g., new VR interfaces) — existing heuristics are insufficient.
When the team has no UX expertise; untrained evaluators produce low-reliability findings.

section.name: nielsens-10-heuristics
section.p: The ten usability heuristics provide a structured checklist for evaluation:

list.item: Visibility of system status — Is user informed of system state and changes in real time?
Match between system and real world — Does it use user language and mental models?
User control and freedom — Can user undo, exit, and recover from mistakes?
Consistency and standards — Is behavior predictable and consistent with platform conventions?
Error prevention — Does it prevent error-prone conditions or present confirmation?
Recognition over recall — Are options visible or is input required from memory?
Flexibility and efficiency — Are there shortcuts or power-user features?
Aesthetic and minimalist design — Is interface clean or cluttered with irrelevant information?
Error recovery — Are error messages helpful with plain language and solutions?
Help and documentation — Is help available and task-focused?

section.name: when-heuristic-eval-fits
section.p: Heuristic evaluation is appropriate for early design review, before usability testing, and when quick assessment is needed. Heuristic evaluation is NOT appropriate when deep user insights are required or when validating with real users is mandatory. Best practice: run heuristic evaluation first, then follow with one round of usability testing to cross-validate severity and surface deeper problems heuristics cannot detect.

rule.statement: Step 1 — Prepare: Define scope (which parts, which flows, which heuristics to focus on), gather interface access and task scenarios, prepare evaluation template.
rule.rationale: Scoping prevents evaluators from getting lost in secondary features; focused scope ensures comprehensive coverage of primary flows.

rule.statement: Step 2 — Select evaluators: Recruit 3–5 with UX expertise. Domain knowledge is helpful but not required.
rule.rationale: Single evaluator finds ~35% of problems; three evaluators find ~60%; five evaluators find ~75%. Diminishing returns beyond five. Diversity of backgrounds increases problem detection.

rule.statement: Step 3 — Conduct independent evaluations: Each evaluator separately explores interface, goes through key tasks, notes heuristic violations, rates severity 0–4. Evaluators must NOT collaborate during evaluation.
rule.rationale: Group evaluation suppresses minority observations and creates groupthink. Independent first, aggregate second is the single most important rule.

rule.statement: Step 4 — Compile findings: Deduplicate across evaluators, aggregate severity ratings, organize by heuristic or location.
rule.rationale: Deduplication prevents counting the same problem multiple times; aggregating severity from multiple raters is more reliable than any single rater's judgment.

rule.statement: Step 5 — Prioritize issues using 0–4 severity scale: 0 = not a problem, 1 = cosmetic, 2 = minor, 3 = major, 4 = catastrophe (fix immediately).
rule.rationale: Severity rating focuses implementation effort on high-impact problems; trivial issues do not deserve development resources.

rule.statement: Step 6 — Report findings with location, heuristic violated, problem description, severity, and specific recommendation.
rule.rationale: Actionable findings with location and severity guide implementation priorities and prevent vague "UI is confusing" feedback.

section.name: severity-scale
section.p: Severity 0: Not a problem or preference-based feedback.
Severity 1: Cosmetic issue; fix if time permits (e.g., icon could be clearer).
Severity 2: Minor issue; low priority to fix (e.g., help link is hard to find).
Severity 3: Major issue; should be fixed before launch (e.g., cannot complete task without confusion).
Severity 4: Catastrophic issue; critical function broken or unusable; fix immediately (e.g., form cannot be submitted).

antipattern.description: Evaluators collaborate during evaluation sessions — brainstorming together or reviewing findings as a group before individual assessments are complete.
antipattern.reason: Collaboration suppresses minority observations. Group discussion creates social pressure to conform; different evaluators' perspectives are the entire value of the method.

antipattern.description: Using only one evaluator to save time or cost.
antipattern.reason: Single evaluator finds only ~35% of problems. Three evaluators find ~60%; the diminishing returns only appear beyond five. Single-evaluator assessments are unreliable.

antipattern.description: Findings are too vague: "UI is confusing" or "buttons are unclear" without location or heuristic.
antipattern.reason: Vague feedback is not actionable. Developers cannot implement changes without knowing which element, which heuristic, and what specific fix is recommended.

antipattern.description: Ignoring severity ratings and implementing all findings equally.
antipattern.reason: All issues are not equal. Severity 1 cosmetic issues do not deserve the same development effort as Severity 4 catastrophic issues. Resources must focus on high-impact problems first.

antipattern.description: Running heuristic evaluation but never acting on findings.
antipattern.reason: Evaluation without implementation is waste. Without visible action on findings, evaluators and team learn that feedback is not valued and stop taking the process seriously.

section.name: heuristic-eval-vs-usability-testing
section.p: Heuristic evaluation uses UX experts; usability testing uses real users. Heuristic evaluation finds violations of standards; usability testing finds real user struggles. Heuristic evaluation is fast and low-cost; usability testing is slower and higher-cost. Heuristic evaluation provides expert opinion; usability testing provides user behavior data. Heuristic evaluation is best for early assessment and quick iteration; usability testing is best for deep understanding. Best practice is both: heuristic evaluation first to clear obvious issues, then usability testing to surface deeper problems and validate severity ratings.

section.name: quick-check-questions
section.p: 1. Does the user always know system status? (Visibility)
2. Is language user-friendly and matched to the real world? (Match)
3. Can user undo and go back? (Control)
4. Is everything consistent with platform conventions? (Consistency)
6. Are options visible or required from memory? (Recognition)
8. Is the interface clean and minimal? (Aesthetic)
9. Do errors help fix problems? (Error Recovery)

template.purpose: Individual evaluator template and compiled report template for aggregating findings from multiple evaluators
